title: Gon, the Little Fox
author: After Niimi Nankichi
paragraphs: A long, long time ago, in a forest beside a quiet little village, there lived a small fox named Gon. Gon had no mother or father, no brothers or sisters. He lived all alone in a burrow beneath the roots of a great oak tree. Because he was so terribly lonely, he would sneak into the village and play tricks on the farmers, stealing sweet potatoes from their fields and trampling their drying peppers just for fun.

One warm afternoon, Gon was trotting along the banks of a stream when he spotted a young fisherman named Hyoju standing knee-deep in the water. Hyoju's wicker basket was full of wriggling eels, their silver bodies catching the sunlight. Gon's eyes went wide with mischief. What a wonderful prank it would be to steal those eels while Hyoju was not looking!

The moment Hyoju turned his back, Gon crept to the basket and snatched every last eel in his jaws. He dashed back into the forest, laughing to himself, his bushy tail held high with pride. He thought himself terribly clever that day.

A few days later, Gon heard the slow, heavy tolling of a bell drifting from the village. Curious, he padded over to see what had happened. White cloth hung from the door of Hyoju's house, and villagers filed in with bowed heads and sorrowful faces. Hyoju's mother had passed away.

Gon hid behind a hedge and watched. Then he overheard two villagers whispering to each other. 'Poor Hyoju caught those eels specially for his sick mother. It was to be her last good meal.' Gon felt as though a thorn had pierced his chest. His ears drooped flat against his head, and his eyes stung with tears he did not know a fox could cry.

'This is all my fault,' Gon whispered as he slunk back into the trees. 'Hyoju's mother wanted those eels more than anything, and I stole them away. How could I have been so thoughtless and cruel?' He walked slowly through the fallen leaves, his small paws heavy with guilt.

From that day on, Gon made a quiet promise to himself. Every morning before the sun rose, he would venture deep into the forest, digging up the fattest chestnuts with his little paws and gathering the freshest mushrooms from the mossy hillside. He would carry them carefully in his mouth and leave them in a neat pile on Hyoju's doorstep while the world still slept.

When Hyoju opened his door that first morning, he stared in wonder at the heap of glossy chestnuts and fragrant mushrooms. 'Who could have left these?' he murmured, looking up and down the path. But there was no one in sight. Hidden in the tall grass across the lane, Gon watched and felt a gentle warmth spreading through his chest.

And so the days passed, one after another. Every morning, without fail, Hyoju found fresh chestnuts and mushrooms waiting for him. Sometimes there were wild berries too, or a small bouquet of mountain wildflowers. Hyoju cooked the chestnuts into sweet rice and simmered the mushrooms into rich, warming soup. Slowly, the sadness in his face began to soften, and a quiet smile returned to his lips.

The villagers talked and wondered. Some said it must be a blessing from the forest spirit. Others guessed a kind neighbor was helping in secret. Hyoju himself could not solve the mystery, but each evening he swept his doorstep clean, as though saying thank you to whoever the gentle giver might be.

One misty autumn morning, Gon padded toward Hyoju's house with an especially large armful of chestnuts. The door was slightly open, so he nudged it with his nose and stepped inside to set the chestnuts on the floor. Just then, Hyoju came around the corner and saw the little fox standing in his doorway.

'You again, fox!' Hyoju cried out in surprise. Gon froze. The chestnuts tumbled from his arms and rolled across the wooden floor in every direction. Hyoju looked at the chestnuts scattered at his feet, then looked at Gon, and in that moment everything became clear.

'Gon... was it you? Were you the one leaving chestnuts at my door all this time?' Hyoju's voice was barely a whisper, trembling with emotion. Gon gazed up into Hyoju's eyes and slowly, gently nodded his small head. A soft, shy smile appeared on the little fox's face.

Hyoju knelt down and gathered Gon into his arms. 'Thank you, Gon. Thank you for watching over me all these days. I am so sorry I did not understand sooner.' Gon nestled his furry head against Hyoju's chest and closed his eyes, feeling a warmth he had never known before. From that day forward, Gon and Hyoju were the very best of friends.

Now the moon hangs high above the village, and the autumn crickets sing their lullaby among the trees. Gon is curled up safely in his cozy den beside Hyoju's house, eyes closed, heart full. He knows that true kindness is always seen in the end, and that it is never too late to show love. So close your eyes now, just like Gon, and let a gentle dream carry you away to that beautiful forest. Goodnight.
